result.chapter_summary: Chapter 4 establishes that the effectiveness of prose is governed by universal psychological principles of language reception, much like tennis strokes are governed by the laws of physics. The author argues that because readability and listenability are functionally identical, the root principles of prose excellence must be found in how the human mind processes connected discourse rather than in isolated words or sentences.

claims.text: A writer can be proficient in composing and revising without knowing the underlying principles that explain their proficiency.
claims.source_passage: A good writer knows how to compose and revise effectively without necessarily knowing the principles behind his proficiency.

claims.text: In prose composition, the only thing that matters is the effective conveyance of meaning during the act of reading.
claims.source_passage: What counts is the effective conveyance of one’s meaning during the time when somebody is reading one’s text.

claims.text: The psychological principles of language reception govern the effectiveness of actual prose.
claims.source_passage: the psychological principles of language reception govern the effectiveness of actual prose.

claims.text: The diversity and contradictions of writing maxims can only be resolved by referring them to root psychological principles.
claims.source_passage: the great diversity and contradictions of writing maxims can be resolved only by referring them to root psychological principles which determine their relations and their importance.

claims.text: Language processing in actual discourse differs significantly from the processing of isolated words and sentences in test situations.
claims.source_passage: actual language processing is different in several respects from the processing of isolated words and sentences in test situations.

claims.text: It is possible to deduce important conclusions about prose difficulty without committing to a single general model of language reception.
claims.source_passage: A more narrow focus on the factors that affect the ease or difficulty of prose will make possible the deduction of some important conclusions without a premature commitment to any single psychological model of language reception.

claims.text: The finding that active sentences are easier to process than passive ones can be reversed when sentences are placed within connected discourse.
claims.source_passage: my own subjective experiments have persuaded me that these results can be reversed in connected discourse, if one places an active sentence amidst a series of passives or an affirmative amidst a series of negative sentences.

claims.text: Listenability and readability are essentially the same functional process.
claims.source_passage: To this concession may be added strong empirical evidence that listenability and readability are the same.

claims.text: There is no absolute functional boundary that can be drawn between oral and written speech.
claims.source_passage: I have already acknowledged in Chapter 1 the impossibility of drawing an absolute, functional boundary between oral and written speech.

claims.text: Experiments involving longer stretches of discourse present significant challenges regarding interpretation and experimental control.
claims.source_passage: But experiments with longer stretches of discourse present the experimenter with grave problems of interpretation and control.

claims.text: The 'Cloze' method of measuring readability via correct word-guesses correlates highly with standard readability formulas.
claims.source_passage: The relative ability of the test group to make correct guesses in different prose samples showed a high correlation with the readability scores of those samples as computed under the standard readability formulas.

claims.text: The efficacy of the Cloze method is rooted in the psychological principles of redundancy and Gestalt pattern completion.
claims.source_passage: Taylor's experiments had two sources of inspiration: information theory and Gestalt psychology.

claims.text: Cloze scores predict reading comprehension and memory more accurately than standard readability formulas.
claims.source_passage: The cloze score seemed to predict more accurately than readability formulas just how much a reader would understand and remember from a prose passage.

claims.text: Standard readability formulas are limited because they can equate stylistic nonsense with simple prose if word length and familiarity are similar.
claims.source_passage: As Taylor observed, readability formulas by themselves will equate Gertrude Stein’s prose with the prose of an elementary-school text, although the relative comprehensibility of the texts will differ enormously.

claims.text: The Cloze method is a more sensitive indicator of readability than standard formulas, even if it is less practical for pre-publication use.
claims.source_passage: No doubt the cloze method is rather impractical as a substitute for readability formulas, but it has proved itself to be a more sensitive indicator of readability than the formulas.

claims.text: Readers often learn most from texts that carry the least 'information' in the technical sense of information theory.
claims.source_passage: The most paradoxical result of the cloze experiments is that readers often learn most from a text which gives them the least 'information' in the technical sense of that word.

claims.text: A text that is technically 'informative' (highly unpredictable) is harder for the mind to assimilate.
claims.source_passage: In fact, the ultimately informative text, in the technical sense, would be nonsense, because the predictability of any deleted word would approach zero.

claims.text: Readability and comprehensibility are enhanced when a text utilizes small, predictable leaps between words, even if this requires using more words.
claims.source_passage: Apparently, therefore, readability and comprehensibility are enhanced if the text makes rather small, predictable leaps from one word to the next, sometimes using five or six words, if necessary, instead of two or three.

claims.text: Oral speech can successfully convey meaning through highly elliptical, technically informative phrasing that would fail in writing.
claims.source_passage: In oral speech, where elliptical phrasing is the norm, one can successfully convey meaning by language so rich in 'information' that it might yield a zero cloze score when transcribed to paper.

claims.text: A text that is ultimately informative in the technical sense would be nonsense because word predictability would approach zero.
claims.source_passage: In fact, the ultimately informative text, in the technical sense, would be nonsense, because the predictability of any deleted word would approach zero.

claims.text: Oral speech frequently uses elliptical phrasing that contains high information density but would yield a zero cloze score if transcribed.
claims.source_passage: In oral speech, where elliptical phrasing is the norm, one can successfully convey meaning by language so rich in “information” that it might yield a zero cloze score when transcribed to paper.

claims.text: Omitting the relative pronoun 'that' can reduce a sentence's local predictability and make it less readable and slower to process.
claims.source_passage: But this sentence is less readable and will take longer to process than its longer version. Omitting the that has reduced its local predictabilities, causing it to get a lower cloze-score.

claims.text: The cloze method is the most accurate method for measuring readability because it factors in wider verbal context, semantic expectations, and syntactic expectations.
claims.source_passage: That is one reason it is the most accurate method now available for measuring readability. A cloze test takes into account the wider verbal context within which the blank space occurs, and therefore factors in both semantic and syntactic expectations.

claims.text: A valid cloze test administration requires presenting a long stretch of unmutilated text before introducing word blanks.
claims.source_passage: Hence, the proper way to administer the cloze test is to present a fairly long stretch of unmutilated text before beginning to blank out individual words.

claims.text: There is a direct connection between the limited capacity of short-term memory and the readability of linguistic clauses.
claims.source_passage: Obviously, there is a connection between this psychological universal and the readability of clauses.

claims.text: Prose that is explicit, repetitious, and jargon-free is easier to predict and thus scores higher on readability tests.
claims.source_passage: Because it is more explicit, repetitious, and jargon-free than the original, its words will be easier to predict.

claims.text: Ease of processing in reading is equivalent to speed of processing.
claims.source_passage: For ease of processing is equivalent to speed of processing.

claims.text: The sequential maneuvers of speech reception occur at an average maximum rate of approximately 200 milliseconds per word.
claims.source_passage: The sequential maneuvers of speech reception are going forward at an average maximum rate of something like 200 milliseconds per word.

claims.text: Because there is a physiological limit to word processing speed, readability is determined by the amount of effort required to process discourse at that fixed rate.
claims.source_passage: Since this approximate rate cannot be exceeded, there is a direct relation between the rate of reception and the amount of effort that goes into processing a given length of discourse.

claims.text: Reading involves a matching process where incoming words are compared against preceding discourse and syntactic-semantic categories.
claims.source_passage: A number of slightly different answers have been proposed, all of which converge on some kind of matching or decision-making activity which is being performed on the incoming words. The words have to be matched with the preceding discourse, and they have to be matched with syntactic-semantic categories.

claims.text: Word predictability speeds up the rate of language processing by facilitating the matching and decision-making activities performed on incoming words.
claims.source_passage: But why does word predictability speed up the rate of process- ing? A number of slightly different answers have been proposed, all of which converge on some kind of matching or decision- making activity which is being performed on the incoming words.

claims.text: If an initial matching attempt for a word fails, the mind must posit further syntactic-semantic categories, slowing the processing rate.
claims.source_passage: But if the fit is bad, we need to posit still further syntactic-semantic categories until we do achieve a good fit.

claims.text: Language processing may require revising previous constructions of a passage to achieve a successful semantic 'fit.'
claims.source_passage: In some cases, we may even need to revise our previous constructions of the passage in order to achieve a successful decision.

claims.text: The cloze method tests the average local predictability of words in their actual context.
claims.source_passage: What the cloze method directly tests is the average local predictability of words in their actual context.

claims.text: Local predictability is a more accurate indicator of processing speed than general word frequency as measured by lists like the Thorndike-Lorge.
claims.source_passage: This local predictability is therefore far more relevant to actual speed of processing than is the general predictability of a word as determined by its position on the Thorndike-Lorge word-frequency list.

claims.text: Cloze measurements are in principle more accurate and informative than traditional readability formulas.
claims.source_passage: That is one reason why cloze measurements are in principle more accurate and informative than any readability formula could be.

claims.text: The speed of syntactical processing depends upon word predictability as much as lexical processing does.
claims.source_passage: Speed of syntactical processing depends upon word predictability just as much as does speed of lexical processing.

claims.text: The grammatical category and function a word is expected to perform are components of its local predictability.
claims.source_passage: The expectation that a word will belong to a certain grammatical category and will perform a certain kind of grammatical function is just as much an element in its local predictability as its expected semantic force.

claims.text: Abnormal syntax and vocabulary can become the norm within the context of a sufficiently long text.
claims.source_passage: Even abnormal syntax and abnormal vocabulary can become the norm for a text that is long enough.

claims.text: The cloze test indicates the processing time required for a text and thus measures its absolute readability.
claims.source_passage: And that is why the cloze test does indicate the processing time that a text will require (and hence its absolute readability) for a given category of readers.

claims.text: Linguistic communication is an ongoing process of uncertainty reduction.
claims.source_passage: The more specific contribution of information theory has been in describing linguistic communication as an ongoing process of uncertainty reduction.

claims.text: The efficiency of language reception depends on decreasing the amount and duration of the reader’s uncertainty throughout a text.
claims.source_passage: the efficiency of language reception depends on decreasing the amount and duration of the reader’s uncertainty wherever possible in a text.

claims.text: The maximum accurate reading rate for easy English prose is approximately 300 words per minute, or ten syllables per second.
claims.source_passage: The maximum accurate reading rate is about 300 words per minute— or about ten syllables per second.

claims.text: The high speeds claimed for speed reading are illusory.
claims.source_passage: The claims made for faster rates in speed reading are illusory

claims.text: An absolute limitation of the human mind's speed of operation is 50 milliseconds per minimal item.
claims.source_passage: An absolute limitation of the mind’s speed of operation is 50 milliseconds per minimal item

claims.text: Any sentence in isolation is semantically uncertain because it can mean whatever it might mean in an indefinite number of actual uses.
claims.source_passage: Any sentence in isolation is semantically uncertain, because it can mean whatever it might mean in an indefinite number of actual uses.

claims.text: Linguistic experiments with isolated words and sentences are unreliable because their inherent semantic uncertainty is almost limitless.
claims.source_passage: That is one reason for the unreliability of some linguistic experiments with isolated words and sentences. Their perceived uncertainty varies from person to person, and their inherent semantic uncertainty is almost limitless.

claims.text: Reaction time in recognizing a stimulus increases linearly as the logarithm of the number of possible stimuli when each has an equal probability.
claims.source_passage: Hick, who demonstrated in 1952 that reaction time increased linearly as the logarithm of the number of possible stimuli, when each stimulus has an equal probability of occurrence.

claims.text: The interpretation of an uncertain signal involves a series of successive matching attempts against plausible signals.
claims.source_passage: the longer time required to interpret an uncertain signal results from a series of successive matching attempts. Apparently, the actual signal is successively matched against the different plausible signals that could occur in that particular situation.

claims.text: Reaction time decreases when the expectancy or predictability of specific signals increases.
claims.source_passage: reaction time will decrease for some signals when the experimenter increases the expectancy or predictability of those particular signals.

claims.text: A cloze test indicates the readability of prose by indicating its average subjective uncertainty and processing speed.
claims.source_passage: All of these results lend support to my speculation that a cloze test will indicate the readability of prose by indicating its average subjective uncertainty, and hence the average speed with which it can be processed.

claims.text: The only way to reduce uncertainty in language is to reduce the number of plausible semantic-syntactic candidates at any given point.
claims.source_passage: the only way to reduce uncertainty is to reduce the number of plausible alternatives which must pass through our matching-monitor before a stretch of discourse is understood. That is to say, we must reduce uncertainty by reducing the number of semantic-syntactic candidates at any point in the discourse.

claims.text: In readable prose, the number of plausible succeeding signals is reduced to a very few, each having a high subjective probability.
claims.source_passage: In readable prose, these will be reduced to a very few, each having a high subjective probability.

claims.text: The function of context in language processing is to impose constraints on the syntactic and semantic possibilities of speech.
claims.source_passage: Its actual function is to impose constraints on the syntactic and semantic possibilities of speech.

claims.text: Contextual constraints are greater in the middle of a discourse than at the beginning due to the sequential character of language.
claims.source_passage: Because of the sequential character of language, these contextual constraints ought to be greater in the middle of a discourse than at the beginning, and this has been confirmed for words as well as for sentences

claims.text: Uncertainty in language processing is reduced by increasing constraint.
claims.source_passage: In the parlance of information theory, we reduce uncertainty by increasing constraint.

claims.text: A large amount of constraint results in small amounts of time and effort required for processing.
claims.source_passage: With this large amount of constraint, the time and effort required for processing will be small.

claims.text: The beginning of a sentence or a discourse should be made very constraining to aid the reader.
claims.source_passage: A directly practical implication of this psychological fact is that the beginning of a sentence, and particularly of a whole discourse, should be made very constraining.

claims.text: Beginnings of discourses have the greatest psychological impact and are remembered more firmly than middles or ends.
claims.source_passage: Beginnings also have the greatest psychological impact; they are more closely attended to and more firmly remembered than middles and ends.

claims.text: Readability is greatly enhanced by increasing the constraining power of beginnings.
claims.source_passage: It follows that the readability of prose will be greatly enhanced by increasing the constraining power of beginnings.

claims.text: Short-term expectancy is a psychological force that can override the long-term expectancy predicted by general probability.
claims.source_passage: The constraint of beginnings has, in addition, a psychological force greater than the average local predictabilities of the ongoing discourse. This is an important phenomenon which I shall call “short-term expectancy.”

claims.text: The human tendency to act on short-term expectancy likely has a basis in biological evolution because it helps humans cope with reality.
claims.source_passage: I believe that our tendency to do so is highly useful in coping with reality and hence might have a basis in biological evolution.

claims.text: Short-term probabilities of local experiences are usually more reliable than general long-term probabilities.
claims.source_passage: This is practically useful to us, because the short-term probabilities of a local experience are usually more reliable than general long-term probabilities.

claims.text: Poetry is often easier to read than prose due to the high predictability of its rhythm.
claims.source_passage: Herbert Spencer suggested that poetry is often easier to read than prose because of the greater predictability of its rhythm. If that is correct, as I believe it is, then the handbooks on composition have paid too little attention to prose rhythm.

claims.text: Actual speech patterns tend to be isochronous, suggesting rhythm is continued in both production and reception.
claims.source_passage: Some studies have indicated that actual speech patterns tend to be isochronous, suggesting that we tend to continue rhythmical patterns in the production as well as in the reception of speech.

claims.text: Written language must explicitly introduce contextual constraints into the text itself to replace the missing situational context.
claims.source_passage: From this I inferred that written language had to introduce these constraints explicitly into the text itself.

claims.text: Excessive explicit contextual constraint makes prose unreadable, similar to legal documents.
claims.source_passage: On the other hand, if all contextual constraints were made explicit, prose would drift towards the unreadability of highly explicit legal documents.

claims.text: Human beings tend to continue rhythmical patterns during both the production and reception of speech.
claims.source_passage: we tend to continue rhythmical patterns in the production as well as in the reception of speech.

claims.text: Short-term expectancies in language are based on pattern continuations induced by local verbal context.
claims.source_passage: Such short-term expectancies, based on pattern continuations, could be termed “small-scale constraints.” They are the expectancies induced by the local verbal context.

claims.text: Large-scale context is more important than small-scale constraints in reducing uncertainty and enhancing readability.
claims.source_passage: the larger-scale context of prose is still more important than small-scale constraints in reducing uncertainty and enhancing readability.

claims.text: Written language lacks the situational context that naturally constrains meaning possibilities in oral speech.
claims.source_passage: one of the distinctive traits of written as opposed to oral speech was the lack in writing of a situational context to constrain meaning possibilities.

claims.text: If every contextual constraint in prose were made explicit, the text would become as unreadable as a legal document.
claims.source_passage: if all contextual constraints were made explicit, prose would drift towards the unreadability of highly explicit legal documents.

claims.text: The majority of contextual constraint in prose remains invisible and covert.
claims.source_passage: Even in prose, therefore, the greater amount of contextual constraint remains invisible, like the base of an iceberg.

claims.text: The invisible context of prose consists of linguistic rules, semantic conventions, and large domains of tacitly shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: It consists of linguistic “rules” and semantic conventions. It embraces large domains of tacitly shared knowledge, and it includes tacit suppositions about the theme and tendency of the text as a whole.

claims.text: A writer's most important decision is assessing what knowledge can be tacitly assumed in their audience.
claims.source_passage: A shrewd decision about the knowledge that the writer can tacitly assume in his audience may be the most important decision the writer makes.

claims.text: The primary cause of unreadable writing is rarely a neglect of large-scale covert constraints.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, a neglect of these large-scale covert constraints is rarely the chief cause of unreadable writing.

claims.text: The main cause of bad writing is small-scale, local uncertainty persisting from word to word and sentence to sentence.
claims.source_passage: Practical experience tells us that the main uncertainties of bad writing are small-scale, local uncertainties, persisting from word to word and sentence to sentence.

claims.text: The time required to process discourse is determined by local uncertainties because uncertainty necessitates more successive mental matching-operations.
claims.source_passage: The time it takes to process a given stretch of discourse is largely determined by its local uncertainties, because, as we have seen, more uncertainties will require more successive matching-operations before the reader experiences a click of understanding.

claims.text: Human mental matching operations are conducted successively rather than simultaneously.
claims.source_passage: It appears, therefore, that we are unable to carry out several matching operations simultaneously and that we must process them successively, one at a time.

claims.text: There is a linear relationship between the amount of uncertainty in a stimulus and the time required to interpret it.
claims.source_passage: experiments since the nineteenth century have consistently shown a linear relation between the amount of our uncertainty and the amount of time we require to interpret a stimulus.

claims.text: The maximum human processing rate for successive mental matchings is approximately twenty items per second.
claims.source_passage: It is now known that the maximum rate for conducting these successive matchings is about twenty items per second.

claims.text: High linguistic expectancy improves processing speed by reducing the number of semantic-syntactic candidates the mind must generate to achieve a fit.
claims.source_passage: If the kind of stimulus we actually receive is the kind we expected, then we will need to generate only a few candidates before achieving a fit.

claims.text: Linguistic processing ceases if the central mental monitor is diverted to nonlinguistic stimuli.
claims.source_passage: if this central monitor turns aside to perform its function on some nonlinguistic stimulus, then linguistic processing comes to a halt.

claims.text: Human attention cannot be paid simultaneously to several discrete objects; instead, it shifts back and forth rapidly.
claims.source_passage: Although the individual objects of attention can be rich and complex, they must nonetheless be unified objects, for we cannot pay attention simultaneously to several discrete objects. Whenever we appear to do so, we are undoubtedly shifting attention back and forth very rapidly

claims.text: High expectancy reduces cognitive fatigue by requiring attention to visit fewer mental 'addresses' per unit of discourse.
claims.source_passage: Attention has to go to fewer addresses per unit of discourse, and therefore gets less tired out.

claims.text: The human linguistic monitor is characterized by a one-thing-at-a-time bottleneck connected to the phenomenon of attention.
claims.source_passage: Thus, our linguistic bottleneck is obviously connected in some way with the phenomenon of attention. The feature of one-thing-at-a-time in our linguistic monitor is the distinctive feature of attention.

claims.text: High linguistic expectancy improves readability by luring attention to the correct semantic address faster than low expectancy.
claims.source_passage: A good way of describing the relation between linguistic uncertainty and speed of processing is to say that high expectancy lures our attention to the right address much sooner than low expectancy does.

claims.text: Unsuccessful shifts of attention caused by local linguistic uncertainties increase the time and effort required for reading.
claims.source_passage: It is obvious that every shift of attention takes time, so that every unsuccessful shift of attention induced by local uncertainties will cost wasted time and effort in the reading process.

claims.text: Prose that fails to control reader attention is inherently arduous and unreadable.
claims.source_passage: It is also obvious that prose which fails to hold and control our attention will be arduous and unreadable prose.

claims.text: Readable prose must minimize the number of attention shifts required between individual words and between sentences.
claims.source_passage: Readable prose must, as we have seen, require few shifts of attention between individual words. It must also require very few shifts of attention between sentences.

claims.text: Thematic focus is a more universal principle of attention-holding than the use of arresting images or metaphors.
claims.source_passage: A more truly universal principle of attention-holding is that of thematic focus. To avoid wasteful attention shifts, the verbal theme of one phrase must be similar to the theme of the preceding phrase.

claims.text: High predictability and fulfillment of expectations in prose eventually result in boredom and a deterioration of attention.
claims.source_passage: Prose that constantly fulfills expectations exactly as the reader thinks that it will, will be boring prose. Very continuous and very high predictability is the essence of boredom.

claims.text: Monotony independently causes a deterioration of attention even when physical fatigue is excluded.
claims.source_passage: This paradoxical phenomenon has been confirmed experimentally: when all possible effects of fatigue are excluded from the computation, monotony, by itself, will cause a deterioration of attention in all kinds of circumstances.

claims.text: Stylistic variety improves readability by reducing reader effort.
claims.source_passage: This fact substantiates Herbert Spencer's suggestion of 1852 that variety in style actually lessens reader effort.

claims.text: Effective readability requires an intelligent compromise between narrow attention constraints and loose variety.
claims.source_passage: It appears, therefore, that readability requires both a narrow constraint on attention and also a looseness of constraint. In good prose, this conflict is resolved by making intelligent compromises between the two opposing factors.

claims.text: The clause is the primary perceptual unit of language, more directly perceived than individual words.
claims.source_passage: Recently, the primacy of the clause has received imposing support from ingenious experiments which have shown that clauses in English are more directly perceived than their constituent individual words!

claims.text: In compound sentences, clause length is the relevant factor determining readability, rather than overall sentence length.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, it has been shown that, in compound sentences, clause length rather than overall sentence length is the relevant factor in readability scores.

claims.text: Readers suspend final decisions regarding the functions of individual words until they achieve semantic closure for the clause.
claims.source_passage: The most probable explanation of the paradox is that we suspend some of our final decisions about the syntactic-semantic functions of the constituent words until after we have decided on the meaning of the clause.

claims.text: Clauses in English are more directly perceived than their constituent individual words.
claims.source_passage: support from ingenious experiments which have shown that clauses in English are more directly perceived than their constituent individual words!

claims.text: Human perception suspends final decisions about the syntactic-semantic functions of constituent words until the meaning of the clause is determined.
claims.source_passage: The most probable explanation of the paradox is that we suspend some of our final decisions about the syntactic-semantic functions of the constituent words until after we have decided on the meaning of the clause.

claims.text: Constituent words and phrases are perceived in a definite way only after semantic closure is achieved.
claims.source_passage: We perceive the constituent words and phrases in a definite way only after we have achieved semantic closure.

claims.text: In language perception, the whole unit is prior to its constituent parts.
claims.source_passage: But the basic insight that the whole is prior to the parts in language perception must be roughly accurate.

claims.text: Language processing involves a reviewing procedure where information passes by the attention-monitor twice.
claims.source_passage: it follows that language processing must entail some kind of reviewing procedure whereby everything must pass by the attention-monitor twice

claims.text: The maximum reading rate of 300 words per minute is exactly half of the human maximum processing rate of twenty times per second.
claims.source_passage: This is just one-half our maximum processing rate of twenty times per second, suggesting that the maximum reading rate even of the easiest prose involves a scanning-plus-reviewing procedure through our attention-monitor.

claims.text: Ideographic reading is not an important factor in language processing speeds.
claims.source_passage: My contention that ideographic reading cannot be an important factor is confirmed by experiments with accelerated oral speech.

claims.text: The clause is the minimal unit that possesses semantic determinacy.
claims.source_passage: The clause, then, is the primary perceptual unit of all languages because it is the minimal unit that has semantic deter- minacy.

claims.text: All languages utilize the clause as a bracketing mechanism to consolidate temporal sequences into perceived semantic units.
claims.source_passage: all languages require the use of a bracketing mechanism—the clause—which consolidates a sequence of elements into a definitely perceived semantic unit.

claims.text: Humans are incapable of producing or receiving speech except through the structure of clauses.
claims.source_passage: We can speak only in clauses, and we can receive speech only in clauses.

claims.text: Complex perception is achieved by integrating items currently in attention with items from the immediate past.
claims.source_passage: We are somehow able to integrate present items of attention with items from the immediate past in order to form a more complex and unified present perception.

claims.text: The human attention-monitor is limited to processing one frame at a time and approximately twenty frames per second.
claims.source_passage: Its limitation to one frame at a time, and to twenty frames per second must be reckoned with, not only for language reception but for perceptual experience in general.

claims.text: Perception requires a mechanism beyond the attention-monitor to allow for the perception of complex wholes.
claims.source_passage: If this attention-monitor were the only mechanism of perception, it would be hard to conceive how we could ever perceive complex wholes.

claims.text: Human perception integrates present items of attention with items from the immediate past to form unified perceptions.
claims.source_passage: We are somehow able to integrate present items of attention with items from the immediate past in order to form a more complex and unified present perception.

claims.text: The processing of a linguistic clause requires a consolidating function that stores unorganized items for retrieval.
claims.source_passage: In order to perceive something all at once, though it passes through our attention system bit by bit, we need to have a consolidating function that supplements our attention-monitor. We need a perceptual depository able to store perceived but not yet organized items and to keep them available on demand.

claims.text: Primary memory makes the subject aware of the 'just past' as part of the present space of time, rather than a recollected past.
claims.source_passage: What elementary memory makes us aware of is the just past... In fact it comes to us as belonging to the rearward portion of the present space of time, and not to the genuine past.

claims.text: The readability of prose discourse is determined by the psychological connection between present perceptions and past perceptions in long-term memory.
claims.source_passage: It is this frontier which largely determines the readability of prose discourse, as distinct from the readability of its individual sentences.

claims.text: Short-term memory has a precisely limited capacity that is more definite in terms of items than duration.
claims.source_passage: The basic discovery is the precisely limited capacity of short-term memory, a limitation which appears to be more definite for the number of items it can store than for the number of seconds during which it can store them.

claims.text: In the context of language, the item limit of short-term memory is approximately five items.
claims.source_passage: (More recent studies suggest that, in language, the limitation of items in short-term memory is closer to five than to seven.)

claims.text: If a clause exceeds short-term memory capacity, the reader must restart the scanning-reviewing process because functional components are forgotten.
claims.source_passage: If the clause requires a reader to exceed the capacity of his short-term memory, then the clause will not be very readable, because some of its functional words or phrases will have been forgotten before the clause terminates, and the reader will have to go through the scanning-reviewing process all over again.

claims.text: Readability is not a simple, direct function of clause length in terms of word count.
claims.source_passage: This general observation certainly does not imply a simple, direct connection between clause length and readability.

claims.text: The limit of short-term memory imposes a corresponding limit on the permissible complexity of grammatical structures in all languages.
claims.source_passage: there is a firm limitation on the permissible complexities of grammatical structure in all languages.

claims.text: Grammatical 'depth' refers to the number of relationships a grammatical structure requires a person to maintain in short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: Victor Yngve, who uses the term depth for the number of relationships which any grammatical structure requires a person to keep in short-term memory.

claims.text: Language processing is a temporal phenomenon.
claims.source_passage: The grammatical structure is revealed and confirmed as the words unfold, but that nontemporal structure could not be revealed unless its unfolding components remained present to the mind.

claims.text: There is a firm limitation on the number of linguistic elements that can remain present to the mind simultaneously.
claims.source_passage: And since there is a firm limitation on the number of elements that can remain present to the mind, there is a firm limitation on the permissible complexities of grammatical structure in all languages.

claims.text: Grammatical depth is determined by the number of hierarchical steps that must precede semantic closure within a clause.
claims.source_passage: Briefly and simply conceived, grammatical depth is determined by the number of hierarchical steps that must precede semantic closure within a clause.

claims.text: Elements operating on the same grammatical plane are less taxing to short-term memory than an equal number of elements in a grammatical hierarchy.
