claims.source_passage: If the reader must constantly reread clauses, sentences, and paragraphs to construe the writer's meaning, he will not understand or remember that meaning very well.

claims.text: The principle of linearity defines the boundary between readable and unreadable prose.
claims.source_passage: So long as the reader can process what he reads without having to circle back and reread an earlier part of the text, then the writing is linear. It is writing that has not crossed over the boundary line into unreadability.

claims.text: Readers store textual meaning primarily in nonlinguistic form while memory for specific linguistic form decays rapidly.
claims.source_passage: Since the reader stores textual meaning mainly in nonlinguistic form, and since his memory for linguistic form begins to decay very rapidly, the only memorable feature of difficult, unlinear prose is the memory of its difficulty.

claims.text: Difficult, non-linear prose results in the reader remembering the difficulty of the reading process rather than the gist of the meaning.
claims.source_passage: the only memorable feature of difficult, unlinear prose is the memory of its difficulty. Its gist would have been better remembered if conveyed in a linear style.

claims.text: Linearity is a capacious principle that permits intricate, deliberate, and long periodic sentences, provided they do not force rereading.
claims.source_passage: The principle of linearity is nonetheless very capacious. It admits highly deliberate writing, intricate and even precious writing. It admits long periodic sentences

claims.text: Linearity is the minimal requirement for all communicative prose.
claims.source_passage: Linear prose is not always easy prose, but linearity is a minimal requirement of communicative prose.

claims.text: Resolving stylistic conflicts requires balancing closure, expectation, and contextualization to ensure the forward movement of the mind.
claims.source_passage: When linearity is taken as a principle for resolving stylistic conflicts, we can conceive of this resolution as a balancing of three factors. (1) Closure... (2) Expectation... (3) Contextualization... these ingredients of linearity should be balanced against each other so that none is altogether neglected.

claims.text: The psychological understanding of language reception is currently very imperfect and limited primarily to words and sentences in isolation.
claims.source_passage: The psychology of language reception is still very imperfectly understood, and the bulk of experimentation in the subject has been limited to words and sentences in isolation.

claims.text: Existing inferences regarding the psychology of readability must be considered tentative and conjectural due to problems of experimental interpretation and control in discourse research.
claims.source_passage: Consequently, despite the great quantity of interesting work in the field, my own inferences from the psychological literature must be at times tentative and conjectural.

claims.text: There is a .96 correlation between Fang's listenability test and the Flesch Reading Ease formula.
claims.source_passage: Fang has demonstrated a .96 correlation between his listenability test and the Flesch Reading Ease formula

claims.text: A reader’s processing speed can be significantly hindered by difficult syntax even when the individual words used are highly predictable.
claims.source_passage: the reader may be slowed down by hard syntax, even when he ultimately finds the words of the text highly predictable.

claims.text: The maximal processing rate for highly familiar syllable sequences is established at ten syllables per second, or 200 milliseconds per two-syllable word.
claims.source_passage: Sperling has shown that the maximal processing rate for highly familiar syllable sequences is about ten syllables per second—again 200 milliseconds per two-syllable word

claims.text: Human subjects tend to act against long-term expectancy in specific contexts by guessing unlikely events in proportion to their actual frequency (e.g., 20%) rather than optimizing for probability.
claims.source_passage: Hake has shown that when subjects are asked to guess which of two events will occur, and they know in advance that the occurrences will be 20% versus 80%, they will guess the unlikely event 20% of the time and the likely event only 80% of the time.

claims.text: Arresting images and metaphors can paradoxically distract a reader from a discourse's primary semantic goals, potentially harming readability.
claims.source_passage: But such flagwaving could in some cases distract attention from the main semantic goals of a discourse.

claims.text: The theoretical insight that the clause is the primary unit of speech was originally identified by Wilhelm von Humboldt.
claims.source_passage: Cassirer, discussing all known language types in his Philosophy of Symbolic Forms in 1953, traced the insight back to von Humboldt.

claims.text: Individual words are perceived more directly by the mind than the individual phonemes that make them up.
claims.source_passage: Some ingenious research has shown that words are more directly perceived than their constituent phonemes.

claims.text: Comprehension drops sharply for oral discourse once speech is accelerated beyond 300 words per minute, and this limitation cannot be improved by training.
claims.source_passage: This maximum rate is 300 words per minute—again ten syllables per second. After that point there is a sharp drop in comprehension that cannot be elevated by training.

claims.text: The recall of syntactic and lexical form is possible for short phrases (approx. 11 words) but typically fails for longer passages (60-86 words) even when meaning is preserved.
claims.source_passage: They discovered that when children were asked to recall short, eleven word passages they could remember the syntactic and lexical form... but, when asked to recall longer passages of sixty to eighty-six words, they remembered the meaning accurately but not the form.

claims.text: Thomas Babington Macaulay's early prose style was characterized by complexity, while his later style shifted toward greater simplicity.
claims.source_passage: To find an example, one can turn to any page of Macaulay’s early prose. (His later style advanced to greater simplicity.)

claims.text: Early German texts, including the Luther Bible, placed verbs in the middle of dependent clauses, indicating the verb-at-the-end rule is a later stylistic imposition.
claims.source_passage: Earlier German dependent clauses—in the Luther Bible, for instance—characteristically put the verb in the middle, just as in independent clauses.

claims.text: The ability of subjects to remember adjectives is more than doubled when the noun is presented before the adjectives rather than after them.
claims.source_passage: More than twice as many adjectives were given back after description I [Secretary who is...] than after description II [Severe, cool... secretary].

evidence.description: Bill Tilden's observation that diverse physical movements in tennis are valid as long as the racket face makes effective contact with the ball.
Studies by Olson and Filby (1972) showing that active sentences are processed more easily than passive ones in isolation.
evidence.source_reference: D. R. Olson and N. Filby, 'On the Comprehension of Active and Passive Sentences,' Cognitive Psychology 3 (1972): 361-81

evidence.description: Fang's research demonstrating a .96 correlation between listenability scores and the Flesch Reading Ease formula.
evidence.source_reference: Fang

evidence.description: Sticht's experiments showing no significant differences between reading and listening comprehension scores within tested groups.
evidence.source_reference: Sticht

evidence.description: The development and testing of the 'Cloze' method using 5th or 6th word deletion to measure predictability.
evidence.source_reference: Taylor, W. L. (1954). 'Application of Cloze and Entropy Measures...'

evidence.description: Demonstration that Cloze scores correlate with individual differences in reading comprehension and aptitude.
evidence.source_reference: Taylor, W. L. (1957). 'Cloze Readability Scores as Indices of Individual Differences...'

evidence.description: The observation that redundancy allows for the immediate recognition of errors like 'Man bites dxg.'
evidence.source_reference: G. A. Miller

evidence.description: Comparison of a sentence with and without the relative pronoun 'that' to show impact on local predictability and readability.
A comparative experiment using a passage from Bormuth and Hirsch's revision of it to show that jargon-free, explicit prose is more predictable in a cloze test.
evidence.source_reference: Bormuth (cited in text)

evidence.description: Research showing that the maximal processing rate for adults is roughly 200ms per word and that speed reading claims are illusory.
evidence.source_reference: E. J. Gibson and H. Levin, The Psychology of Reading (1975)

evidence.description: Research demonstrating the maximal processing rate for highly familiar material.
evidence.source_reference: Sperling (cited in text)

evidence.description: Study on the recall of meaningful material and the role of verbal context.
evidence.source_reference: G. A. Miller and J. A. Selfridge, 'Verbal Context and the Recall of Meaningful Material' (1950)

evidence.description: Research showing that adults read at a maximum of 300 words per minute and that speed reading claims are unfounded.
evidence.source_reference: E. J. Gibson and H. Levin, The Psychology of Reading (1975)
evidence.source_passage: The maximum reading rate for adults is about 300 words per minute... The claims made for faster rates in speed reading are illusory (see E. J. Gibson and H. Levin, The Psychology of Reading, [Cambridge, Mass., 1975], pp. 539-49).

evidence.description: Study showing a maximal processing rate of ten syllables per second for familiar sequences.
evidence.source_reference: G. Sperling, 'A Model for Visual Memory Tasks' (1969)
evidence.source_passage: Sperling has shown that the maximal processing rate for highly familiar syllable sequences is about ten syllables per second—again 200 milliseconds per two-syllable word

evidence.description: Establishment of the 50ms per item limit in psychobiology.
evidence.source_reference: A. B. Kristofferson, 'Attention and Psychophysical Time' (1967)
evidence.source_passage: An absolute limitation of the mind’s speed of operation is 50 milliseconds per minimal item (see A. B. Kristofferson, “Attention and Psychophysical Time,” Acta Psychologica 27 [1967]: 93-100.)

evidence.description: Grammatically ambiguous sentence used to demonstrate uncertainty in processing.
evidence.source_reference: General linguistic usage
evidence.source_passage: Flying airplanes can be dangerous, where the example is understood to be gramatically uncertain or ambiguous

evidence.description: Demonstration that reaction time increases linearly as the logarithm of the number of possible stimuli.
evidence.source_reference: W. E. Hick, 'On the Rate of Gain of Information' (1952)
evidence.source_passage: Hick, who demonstrated in 1952 that reaction time increased linearly as the logarithm of the number of possible stimuli, when each stimulus has an equal probability of occurrence.

evidence.description: The example 'Flying airplanes can be dangerous' shows how a sentence can be grammatically stable yet semantically and grammatically ambiguous.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (General Linguistic Example)
evidence.source_passage: Flying airplanes can be dangerous, where the example is understood to be gramatically uncertain or ambiguous, even though the surface grammar is stable.

evidence.description: Studies showing reaction time decreases when the expectancy of signals is increased by the experimenter.
evidence.source_reference: E. R. F. W. Crossman, 'Entropy and Choice Time' (1953)
evidence.source_passage: reaction time will decrease for some signals when the experimenter increases the expectancy or predictability of those particular signals.

evidence.description: Broadbent's explanation of response bias correlating reaction time with subjective probability.
evidence.source_reference: D. E. Broadbent, 'Word Frequency Effect and Response Bias'
evidence.source_passage: Broadbent, in a classic article, proposed an explanation of this 'response bias' by suggesting a correlation between reaction time and the subject’s sense of a signal’s probability.

evidence.description: G. A. Miller's work showing contextual constraints are greater in the middle of discourse than at the beginning for words and sentences.
evidence.source_reference: G. A. Miller, G. A. Heise, and W. Lichter (1951); G. A. Miller and S. Isard (1963)

evidence.description: A test sentence demonstrating that subjects prefer a word that fits a local rhythmic pattern even if it violates general frequency expectations (long-term expectancy).
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (test sentences involving 'cops' vs 'police')

evidence.description: Hake's study on frequency perception where subjects guess unlikely events 20% of the time when told the ratio is 20/80, rather than guessing the 80% choice every time.
evidence.source_reference: H. W. Hake, 'The Perception of Frequency of Occurrence' (1955)

evidence.description: Studies indicating that speech patterns are isochronous (regularly timed).
evidence.source_reference: Cited in endnote 15 (though text does not name author)

evidence.description: Description of covert semantic assumptions and presuppositions as a field for research.
evidence.source_reference: Carroll and Freedle, Language Comprehension and the Acquisition of Knowledge

evidence.description: Large variations in cloze-test scores among different classes of readers confirm the role of covert contextual constraints.
Experiments since the 19th century showing a linear relation between uncertainty and interpretation time.
Studies identifying the maximum processing rate of twenty items per second.
evidence.source_reference: Referenced as note 6 and 17 in text

evidence.description: Polyphonic music as an example of shifting attention back and forth between themes.
Experimental findings that monotony causes attention deterioration even when fatigue is removed from the calculation.
evidence.source_reference: Broadbent, Perception and Communication (1958), pp. 135-38

evidence.description: Experimental evidence that clauses are perceived more directly than constituent words in English.
Readability score analysis showing clause length is more relevant than sentence length in compound sentences.
Experiments with accelerated oral speech showing a sharp drop in comprehension after 300 words per minute.
evidence.source_reference: D. B. Orr, H. L. Friedman, and J. C. Williams (1965)

evidence.description: The arithmetical correlation between maximum processing rate (20 frames/sec) and maximum reading rate (10 syllables/sec).
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (citing general psychological data)

evidence.description: William James's description of 'primary' or 'elementary' memory as the directly intuited past.
evidence.source_reference: W. James, Principles of Psychology (New York, 1890)
evidence.source_passage: In 1890 William James called it “elementary” or “primary” memory and described its essential character better than anyone since

evidence.description: Miller's identification of the 'Magic Number Seven' as the limit of human information processing capacity.
evidence.source_reference: G. A. Miller, 'The Magic Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two' (1956)
evidence.source_passage: The most famous and interesting account of this limitation is Miller’s article “The Magic Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two.”

evidence.description: Research adjusting the item limit in short-term memory specifically for language tasks to approximately five items.
evidence.source_reference: Broadbent, 'The Magic Number Seven' in Studies in Long Term Memory (1975)
evidence.source_passage: More recent studies suggest that, in language, the limitation of items in short-term memory is closer to five than to seven.

evidence.description: Yngve's theoretical model linking grammatical structure to short-term memory limits via the concept of 'depth'.
evidence.source_reference: Victor Yngve
evidence.source_passage: This principle has been ably argued in a brilliant and pathbreaking article by Victor Yngve, who uses the term depth for the number of relationships which any grammatical structure requires a person to keep in short-term memory.

evidence.description: The contrast between the regressive phrase 'a certainly not very clearly defined color' and a rearranged version with lower memory load.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch/Yngve

evidence.description: Correlations between hesitation pauses/intonation breaks and phrase structure boundaries.
evidence.source_reference: D. S. Boomer, “Hesitation and Grammatical Encoding,” 1965

evidence.description: Evidence that Yngve depth increases processing time.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (citing general consensus/unnamed studies)

evidence.description: Serial processing of sense and syntax increasing memory load in hierarchical structures.
evidence.source_reference: Forster and Ryder (cited in text)

evidence.description: Experiment involving the identification of curved lines based on verbal descriptions (regressive vs progressive structure).
evidence.source_reference: Rommetveit (cited in text)

evidence.description: Forster and Ryder's study showing that semantic and syntactic hypotheses are processed serially.
evidence.source_reference: K. I. Forster and L. A. Ryder, 'Perceiving the Structure and Meaning of Sentences,' Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior 10 (1971): 285-96

evidence.description: Rommetveit's curved line experiment comparing 'A leftward, jaggedly descending broken curve' vs 'A jagged, broken curve, descending leftward.'
evidence.source_reference: R. Rommetveit, Words, Meanings, and Messages (New York, 1968), pp. 287-300.

evidence.description: Rommetveit's secretary experiment comparing adjective lists before and after the noun 'secretary.'
evidence.source_reference: R. Rommetveit, Words, Meanings, and Messages (New York, 1968)

evidence.description: George Miller's research on 'unitizing' or 'chunking' in short-term memory.
evidence.source_reference: George Miller (referenced as 'unitizing')

evidence.description: Comparison of word sequences: 'a color certainly not defined very clearly' vs 'a certainly not again nearly designed color.'
evidence.source_reference: Yngve (referenced example)

evidence.description: George Miller's research on chunking and the limits of communication.
evidence.source_reference: G. A. Miller, The Psychology of Communication (Harmondsworth, 1970), chap. 2

evidence.description: Analysis of a non-sensical word sequence ('a certainly not again nearly designed color') to show memory load resulting from lack of stable groupings.
evidence.source_reference: Based on Victor Yngve's model

evidence.description: A sentence from Macaulay's essay on Dryden (1828) used to demonstrate how periodic sentences maintain readability through predictability.
evidence.source_reference: Macaulay, 'Essay on Dryden' (1828)

evidence.description: Reference to the Luther Bible to show historical German syntax placed verbs in the middle of clauses.
evidence.source_reference: Luther Bible

evidence.description: Observation that the verb-at-the-end rule is absent in contemporary German oral dialects.
evidence.source_reference: General linguistic observation cited by author

evidence.description: T.G. Bever's model of speech perception, which posits the clause as the primary perceptual unit and describes the recoding of clauses into abstract forms.
evidence.source_reference: Bever, 'Perception, Thought and Language,' in Carroll and Freedle, Language Comprehension and the Acquistition of Knowledge, p. 104.
evidence.source_passage: The clause is the primary perceptual unit; (2) within each clause we assign semantic relations between major phrases; (3) after each clause is processed, it is recoded into a relatively abstract form

evidence.description: St. Augustine's phenomenological description of repeating a psalm, which illustrates the shift from expectation to memory through a 'marking faculty.'
evidence.source_reference: St. Augustine, Confessions
evidence.source_passage: I am about to repeat a psalm that I know... So the life of this action of mine extends both ways—into my memory... and into my expectation.

evidence.description: Augustine's introspective analysis of reciting a psalm, illustrating how memory and expectation unify an action into a nontemporal whole.
Experimental findings that children remember the form of short (11 word) passages but only the meaning of long (60-86 word) passages.
evidence.source_reference: A. Binet, and V. Henri, 'La mémoire des phrases (mémoire des idées),' 1894

evidence.description: Recognition tests showing subjects fail to detect lexical substitutions like 'closed' for 'not open', suggesting memory stores 'gist'.
evidence.source_reference: S. Fillenbaum, 'Memory for Gist: Some Relevant Variables,' 1966

evidence.description: Subjects were unable to recognize if a sentence was active or passive after 12 seconds, despite excellent semantic recall.
evidence.source_reference: J. S. Sachs, 'Recognition Memory for Syntactic and Semantic Aspects of Connected Discourse,' 1967

evidence.description: Recognition tests for sentences in a story showed subjects recalled actives as passives and vice versa with equal frequency.
evidence.source_reference: P. N. Johnson-Laird, 'The Perception and Memory of Sentences,' 1970

evidence.description: Experiments on the tendency of subjects to recall synonyms rather than original words, excluding the 'linguistic trace' theory.
evidence.source_reference: Brewer (cited in endnote 41)

evidence.description: Studies on the decay of verbal memory for clauses after 12 seconds.
evidence.source_reference: Johnson-Laird, 'The Perception and Memory of Sentences' (1970); Levelt and Kempen (1975)

evidence.description: Experimental data showing reading rate and retention correspond to the number of propositions in a text.
evidence.source_reference: Kintsch and Keenan (1973)

evidence.description: Finding that each additional proposition adds 1.5 seconds to reading time.
evidence.source_reference: Kintsch and Keenan (1973)

evidence.description: Experiments using history and science texts to measure reading speed and recall based on the density of word concepts (tags) relative to propositions.
evidence.source_reference: W. Kintsch et al., 'Comprehension and Recall of Text as a Function of Content Variables,' Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior 14 (1975): 196-214.
evidence.source_passage: This report was based on a number of different experiments conducted on different kinds of prose texts: short and long texts from history books, and short and long texts from Scientific American.

evidence.description: A statistical table showing a 20% to 105% increase in reading time when many thematic tags are used instead of few.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch, Table 1 based on Kintsch (1975)
evidence.source_passage: If one takes the median of these percentage increases, one gets a striking 32 percent increase in reading time per proposition recalled

evidence.description: A comparative revision of a letter to the editor from a college paper, demonstrating how tag reduction improves readability without changing propositional content or word count.
Research on the representation of meaning in memory, specifically regarding synonymous passages and thematic tags.
evidence.source_reference: Kintsch, Walter. The Representation of Meaning in Memory (1974)
evidence.source_passage: Kintsch has, in fact, made a start on such research. See Kintsch, The Representation of Meaning in Memory, pp. 107-22.

evidence.description: Psycholinguistic discussion of the concept of 'emphasis' in language structure and function.
evidence.source_reference: Halliday, M. A. K. 'Language Structure and Language Function' in New Horizons in Linguistics (1970)
evidence.source_passage: A good discussion of emphasis from a psycholinguistic standpoint can be found in M. A. K. Halliday, “Language Structure and Language Function,” in New Horizons in Linguistics, ed. J. Lyons (Harmondsworth, 1970), pp. 140-65.

evidence.description: A comparison between Lincoln's original Gettysburg Address and a simplified version to demonstrate the loss of semantic adequacy.
A statistical/structural count of phrasal interruptions in the Gettysburg Address (e.g., 'brought forth [on this continent] a new nation').
A list of thematic tags used across 10 sentences of the Gettysburg Address (e.g., nation, conceived, dedicated, war).
Ragnar Rommetveit's study showing that a complex description of a curve ('A leftward, jaggedly descending broken curve') was better remembered than a broken-up version.
Psycholinguistic experiments comparing sentences with and without explicit relative pronouns ('who' or 'which').
evidence.source_reference: Fodor and Garrett (1966); Hakes and Cairns (1970)

evidence.description: Experiments comparing 'complement constructions' with and without the marking word 'that'.
evidence.source_reference: Hakes (1972)

evidence.description: A contrived narrative about Thomas Jefferson and the Rotunda, demonstrating that extreme syntactic integration results in a 118-word sentence that is harder to process than moderate sentence breaks.
A thought experiment contrasting an 'overly short' and an 'overly explicit' version of a sentence about the Nile to demonstrate the need for a middle ground in semantic constraint.
evidence.source_reference: Hirsch (author-generated example)

evidence.description: Alan Moorehead's original sentence from 'The White Nile' is cited as a successful example of balancing speed of closure with necessary semantic constraints.
evidence.source_reference: A. Moorehead, The White Nile (New York, 1960), p. 1.
evidence.source_passage: No unexplored region in our times, neither the heights of the Himalayas, the Antarctic wastes, nor even the hidden side of the moon, has excited quite the same fascination as the mystery of the sources of the Nile.

evidence.description: Studies on the effects of reducing complement constructions and relative pronouns on sentence comprehension.
evidence.source_passage: D. T. Hakes, “Effects of Reducing Complement Constructions on Sentence Comprehension,” Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior 11 (1972): 278-86.

evidence.description: The style of Thomas Babington Macaulay is cited as an example of extreme lucidity that achieves clarity at the expense of factual nuance.
evidence.source_reference: Brand Blanshard, Philosophical Style (1954)

concepts.author_definition: The foundational cognitive mechanisms of language reception that determine the effectiveness and relative importance of practical writing maxims.

The philosophical principle that the only thing that matters in a technical skill (like tennis or writing) is the functional moment of contact or conveyance, rather than the specific technique used to achieve it.
The ease with which spoken language is understood; used here as an empirical proxy for readability.
A system for measuring readability based on the percentage of correct guesses test groups make when every fifth or sixth word is replaced with a blank space.
concepts.common_alternative: Readability is usually measured by surface formulas counting syllable and sentence length.
concepts.source_passage: Instead of measuring word familiarity and sentence length, his method measured the percentage of correct guesses which a test group made when blank spaces were inserted in a prose sample to replace every fifth or sixth word.

concepts.author_definition: The amount of a message that can be deleted without changing its conveyed meaning; an indicator of predictability.
A Gestalt psychology concept referring to the completion of a pattern into an organized form that is subjectively perfected.
concepts.source_passage: Gestalt psychologists called such completion 'closure,' because the result was an organized form which seemed subjectively perfected or closed.

concepts.author_definition: The reduction of uncertainty; a measure of how unpredictable a specific word or element is in a sequence.
concepts.source_passage: Information, according to information theory, is the reduction of uncertainty, and if a word in a text is so highly predictable that it will be guessed even if deleted, then that word carries very little information.

concepts.author_definition: A method of measuring readability by blanking out words in a text to determine their predictability based on semantic and syntactic context.
The probability of a specific word appearing based on the immediate surrounding words or syntax (e.g., the presence of a relative pronoun).
The cognitive facility with which a reader can match incoming words to mental categories, which is functionally equivalent to the speed of processing.
The psychological process of resolving linguistic uncertainty (grammatical or semantic) during the reception of language.
The specific processing time a text requires for a given category of readers, measured by objective indicators like the cloze test.
The functional operation of context that reduces the number of semantic-syntactic candidates at any point in discourse by increasing the subjective probability of a few signals.
concepts.common_alternative: Commonly called 'context,' which Hirsch views as a vague term that fails to describe the actual function of processing.
concepts.source_passage: Constraint is a precise, functional term for context... Its actual function is to impose constraints on the syntactic and semantic possibilities of speech.

concepts.author_definition: The psychological tendency for subjects to recognize signals faster when they perceive them to have a higher probability of occurrence.
The psychological tendency to predict the continuation of a very recent, local pattern even if it differs from long-term general probabilities.
Expectancies induced by the local verbal context, such as rhythmical pattern continuations.
A quality of speech where patterns tend to occur at equal time intervals, contributing to rhythmic predictability.
The vast amount of covert linguistic rules, semantic conventions, and tacitly shared knowledge that constrains meaning without being explicitly stated in the text.
The absolute limit on the speed of linguistic processing (approx. 20 items/sec) caused by the requirement that matching operations be performed successively by the attention monitor.
A linguistic monitor characterized by 'one-thing-at-a-time' processing; it can process complex but only unified objects.
The distinctive feature of human attention where discrete objects cannot be processed simultaneously, creating a linguistic bottleneck.
A principle of attention-holding where the verbal theme of one phrase is similar to the theme of the preceding phrase to fulfill reader expectations.
Continuous and very high predictability in prose that causes the deterioration of reader attention independently of fatigue.
A two-pass sequence in language processing: the first pass perceives undetermined linguistic functions, and the second pass perceives a holistic semantic unit.
The minimal linguistic unit (the clause) that possesses enough semantic determinacy to be perceived as a whole prior to its parts.
A perceptual depository that stores unorganized items from the immediate past, keeping them available to be integrated with the present moment into a complex whole.
concepts.common_alternative: Often conflated with long-term memory or seen as a simple storage bin for information.

concepts.author_definition: A metric representing the quantity of syntactic relationships or structural elements a reader must hold simultaneously in short-term memory to process a clause.
A structure where the mind must 'go back' to previous elements to integrate the current word, creating high memory load.
A structure where grammatical subordinations are fewer and semantic closure is more immediate, reducing memory load.
The view that the mind pursues hypotheses about sense and syntax one after the other rather than simultaneously.
The process by which multiple words are grouped into single semantic-syntactic units held in memory suspension.
The presence of multiple possible 'meaning-candidates' for words or structures before semantic closure is reached.
A sentence structure that delays the completion of its main meaning or syntax until the end, potentially straining memory unless mitigated by subclausal closure.
A syntactical rule (like German verb-at-the-end) that is inefficient for psychological processing and is only preserved through artificial standardisation in a grapholect, not in natural oral speech.
The final mental representation of a text's meaning where temporal/word-order sequences are collapsed into a holistic, 'nontemporal' understanding.
The psycholinguistic boundary or connection point where currently processed linguistic perceptions are integrated with earlier discourse already stored in long-term memory.
A term borrowed from St. Augustine referring to the active mental process that mediates between future expectation and past memory during sequential action.
A model where information is stored in an abstract, nonverbal, and nonsequential form rather than as linguistic or syntactic structures.
concepts.common_alternative: The linguistic assumption that memory stores some form of 'linguistic object' or syntactic trace.

concepts.author_definition: The process of unifying the current meaning being processed with the remembered meaning of the preceding discourse and the expected meaning of the future discourse.
A small number of repeated verbal markers that represent the holistic meaning of a text, aiding the reader's memory integration.
An explicit verbal representation of many implicit meanings; the visible part of a 'semantic iceberg' used to manage complex meanings in discourse.
concepts.source_passage: What I have called a thematic tag is an explicit verbal representation of many implicit meanings. The tag is the visible part of a semantic iceberg.

concepts.author_definition: The psychological device of using a part or a short symbol to stand for a complex whole, allowing the mind to process complexities it could not otherwise attend to all at once.
concepts.source_passage: Instead of trying to think about a whole meaning in its complex fullness and in its relation to other complexities, we isolate a part (or we use a short symbol) in order to represent the whole.

concepts.author_definition: A unit of content analysis used by Kintsch to quantify the informational density of a passage independently of word count or sentence difficulty.
concepts.source_passage: Their special form of content analysis consisted of counting the number of propositions in the passages according to a shrewdly designed system for identifying the propositions.

concepts.author_definition: The underlying semantic units of meaning in a text, independent of its surface word count, often identified through a schematic analysis of predicates and arguments.
concepts.common_alternative: In casual use, propositions are often confused with sentences, but Hirsch/Kintsch distinguish them as base structures.
concepts.source_passage: Their special form of content analysis consisted of counting the number of propositions in the passages according to a shrewdly designed system for identifying the propositions.

concepts.author_definition: Connective words (e.g., 'however', 'thus') that forecast the semantic relationship between a current clause and preceding or following text.
The degree to which the intended meaning is fully captured by the surface text, preventing the 'danger of leaving too much unsaid.'
The degree to which the prose conveys the full range of the writer's intended meaning and implications (e.g., biblical majesty, metaphors).
Linguistic markers, such as the start of a new sentence or theme, that guide the reader's orientation and relationship-sorting in memory.
Breaks in prose (like sentence endings) that signal a shift in theme and allow the reader to integrate previous information before moving on.
The ethical dilemma regarding how far a writer should compromise their complex semantic intentions for the sake of making a style more readable for the audience.
A condition of prose where the reader can process the text continuously in one direction without having to 'circle back' or reread earlier parts to grasp the meaning.
A state of Gestalt closure where a perceived form is completed by the mind and felt to be finished or perfected.
The invisible, shared presuppositions and tacit knowledge between a writer and an audience that constrain the meaning of a text.
The psychological requirements and limitations governing how a reader holds and shifts focus over individual words, sentences, and long stretches of discourse.
The multi-step structural arrangement of phrases (regressive or progressive) that determines the depth and memory load required for processing a clause.

cases.name: Bill Tilden's Tennis Stroke
cases.description: Tilden, a great tennis player, argued that despite various contradictory maxims about footwork and movement, the only essential factor was the racket face's contact with the ball. Hirsch uses this as an analogy for writing: many maxims exist, but only the effective conveyance of meaning during reading truly matters.
key_passages: the only thing that matters is the movement of the racket face during the split second when it stays in contact with the ball. How the successful motion is achieved is secondary.

cases.name: Legal documents
cases.description: Used as a negative example of what happens when a writer attempts to make every contextual constraint explicit. The result is unreadability due to excessive explicitness.
key_passages: if all contextual constraints were made explicit, prose would drift towards the unreadability of highly explicit legal documents.

cases.name: German Syntax Evolution
cases.description: The author tracks German dependent clauses from the Luther Bible (verb-in-middle) to the modern grapholect (verb-at-end), arguing the current rule was an artificial imposition by 17th-18th century planners that violates natural linguistic efficiency (Darwinian/Zipfian conditions).
key_passages: Unquestionably, the verb-at-the-end rule would have been banished from all long clauses in German if the language planners of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries had not permanently imposed their views of correctness on the German language.
In the present day, the verb-at-the-end rule holds in none of the oral dialects of German. It is a psychological and linguistic dinosaur preserved only in the rules of the German grapholect

cases.name: Macaulay's Prose
cases.description: The prose of Thomas Babington Macaulay, specifically his earlier periodic style, is used to show that long, complex sentences can remain readable if they provide the reader with continuous 'subclausal' closure and high predictability.
key_passages: Such a sentence is readable because it creaks with devices of predictability, making every first guess about word groupings the right guess
They do this by subclausal groupings and by intrasentence groupings which have the effective semantic closure of complete sentences.

cases.name: The German Grapholect
cases.description: The author uses the German written language as an example of how artificial syntactic rules (like the verb-at-the-end rule in independent clauses) can persist in a grapholect despite being abandoned by all oral dialects. He characterizes this rule as a 'dinosaur' that hinders the language's efficiency as a communicative tool because it delays closure.
key_passages: In the present day, the verb-at-the-end rule holds in none of the oral dialects of German. It is a psychological and linguistic dinosaur preserved only in the rules of the German grapholect, much to the detriment of that language as an effective instrument of prose.

cases.name: Augustine's Psalm Recitation
cases.description: Augustine describes the mental state of reciting a known psalm. As he speaks, the 'expectation' of the future part is shortened while the 'memory' of the past part is enlarged, yet the entire action exists as a unified, nontemporal whole in his mind.
key_passages: So that, while this marking faculty continues to to work diligently, the expectation is shortened and the memory enlarged, until the whole expectation at length vanishes, and the whole action now ended, shall be altogether passed into the memory.

cases.name: Legal Language (per stirpes)
cases.description: Legal documents use specific terms like 'per stirpes' as thematic tags that represent entire traditions of legal interpretation without needing to explain the complex implicit meanings in every instance.
key_passages: A legal tag like per stirpes, for instance, represents this tradition of legal interpretation as an implication of the tag’s use in a legal document.

cases.name: Literary Characters (James Bond)
cases.description: In a novel, the name of a character serves as a thematic tag for an elaborate system of traits established throughout the text, allowing the writer to reference the whole complexity via the name alone.
key_passages: In a novel, the mere name of a character, say James Bond, is a thematic tag for an elaborate system of character traits that needn’t be rehearsed every time Bond is used in a sentence.

cases.name: Greek Art vs. Babylonian Garden Passages
cases.description: An experiment comparing two passages of similar length and proposition count (8 propositions each). The Greek passage used only 3 thematic tags (repeated), while the Babylonian passage used 8 unique tags. The results showed that the Greek passage was easier to integrate and recall.
key_passages: The Greeks loved beautiful art. When the Romans conquered the Greeks, they copied them, and thus learned to create beautiful art. (21 words, 8 propositions)
The Babylonians built a beautiful garden on a hill. They planted lovely flowers, constructed fountains and designed a pavilion for the queen’s pleasure. (23 words, 8 propositions)
Arguments (thematic tags): GREEK, ART, ROMAN... Arguments (thematic tags): BABYLONIAN, HILL, GARDEN, FLOWER, FOUNTAIN, PAVILION, QUEEN, PLEASURE.

cases.name: Letter-to-the-Editor Revision
cases.description: Hirsch analyzes a letter from a college paper to demonstrate how reducing the variety of word concepts (tags) without losing propositions improves clarity.
key_passages: The article in yesterday’s issue has prompted us to offer a clarification of our actions. We hope to eliminate any confusion which might have developed as a result of the strong similarity between our campaign literature and that used by the current administration in last year’s election
Here is a revision that reduces the number of thematic tags without reducing the number of propositions

cases.name: The College Paper Letter Revision
cases.description: Hirsch selects a letter to the editor from a current college paper and provides a 47-word revision. The original has higher thematic tag density; the revision reduces the number of tags without losing propositions, theoretically improving ease of integration.
key_passages: I shall take the first letter-to-the-editor in today’s college paper... Here is a revision that reduces the number of thematic tags without reducing the number of propositions or changing the number of words.

cases.name: The Gettysburg Address
cases.description: Lincoln's speech is used as a prime example of prose that balances psychological readability with high semantic adequacy. Hirsch analyzes its phrasal units, progressive unfolding, and use of thematic tags to show why it is easy to process despite its stylistic complexity.
key_passages: Each phrasal unit is understood as a unit that requires no mental revision. Each clause unfolds progressively (in Yngve’s sense).
The repetition of thematic tags assures that the connections between the sentences will be attended to and remembered

cases.name: Rommetveit's Curve Experiment
cases.description: An experiment testing the recall of two descriptions of a geometric shape: 'A leftward, jaggedly descending broken curve' vs 'A jagged, broken curve, descending leftward'. The study found the more 'taxing' single description was better remembered.
key_passages: His example was: A leftward, jaggedly descending broken curve, which was better remembered than: A jagged, broken curve, descending leftward.

cases.name: The Jefferson Reconstruction Example
cases.description: Hirsch provides a series of short, choppy sentences about Thomas Jefferson and then contrasts them with a single, highly integrated sentence. He uses this to show that over-integration leads to a 'paradoxical effect of disunity'.
key_passages: Thomas Jefferson, author of the Declaration of Independence is depicted on one side... unlike the previous reconstruction, which failed to follow Jefferson’s plans, when the reconstruction was undertaken after the Rotunda burnt down almost a century ago.

cases.name: Jefferson's Birthday/Rotunda Revision
cases.description: The author presents a poorly constructed sentence merging Jefferson's birthday and the reopening of the Rotunda without a thematic pause, then shows how splitting the sentence improves semantic integration.
key_passages: Jefferson’s birthday will also be celebrated at the University of Virginia. There, the Rotunda, designed by Jefferson, will be reopened... .

cases.name: The White Nile stylistic comparison
cases.description: A comparison of three versions of a sentence describing the fascination of the Nile: one too terse, one too explicit, and Moorehead's balanced original. This demonstrates the trade-offs between brevity and clarity.
key_passages: Nothing is to be found in the longer version that was not implicit in the shorter. But for almost any audience beyond the seventh year of schooling, the second version gives many explicit contexts which... his audience already knew.

thinkers.name: E. B. White
thinkers.key_idea: Writing proficiency is an intuitive skill similar to driving a car, which can be performed without understanding the mechanical principles behind it.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses White's analogy to distinguish between the practical skill of writing and the philosophical/scientific study of the principles that make it work.
key_passages: E. B. White said that he learned to write prose as he learned to drive a car—without understanding what went on under the hood.

thinkers.name: William Tilden
thinkers.key_idea: Diverse and seemingly 'wrong' techniques are valid if the core functional requirement (ball-racket contact) is met.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch elevates Tilden's insight to a 'philosophical principle' that he applies to the analysis of prose composition.
key_passages: William Tilden, the great tennis player, was a more philosophical student of his craft than E. B. White.

thinkers.name: Wilson L. Taylor
thinkers.key_idea: Developed the 'Cloze' method for measuring readability through context-based guessing rather than word/sentence formulas.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch introduces Taylor's method as a departure from traditional readability formulas, focusing on the processing of discourse.
key_passages: Wilson L. Taylor, a doctoral student in psychology, developed a system for measuring readability that was quite unlike any of the standard formulas.

thinkers.name: Gertrude Stein
thinkers.key_idea: Her prose serves as a limit case for readability formulas because its structural simplicity masks its conceptual difficulty.
thinkers.engagement: Used to demonstrate the failures of standard formulas to capture true comprehensibility.
key_passages: As Taylor observed, readability formulas by themselves will equate Gertrude Stein’s prose with the prose of an elementary-school text, although the relative comprehensibility of the texts will differ enormously.

thinkers.name: G. A. Miller
thinkers.key_idea: The relationship between verbal context and the recall of meaningful material; provided examples of how minor syntactic changes affect readability.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Miller's own writing and research to illustrate principles of predictability and processing.
key_passages: Here is a sentence from G. A. Miller, an excellent stylist, whose work happens to be on my desk: In English we recognize immediately that an error has occurred if we read in our newspaper, 'Man bites dxg.'

thinkers.name: Bormuth
thinkers.key_idea: Early research into readability formulas and language variables.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch critiques Bormuth's original prose for being jargon-heavy and less readable, using it as a counter-example to show how revision improves predictability.
key_passages: For the sake of brevity, I will compare the short passage from Bormuth with my revision of it... Because it is more explicit, repetitious, and jargon-free than the original, its words will be easier to predict.

thinkers.name: E. J. Gibson and H. Levin
thinkers.key_idea: The psychology of reading and the limits of reading speed.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their research to debunk speed-reading claims and establish a baseline for adult processing speed.
key_passages: The claims made for faster rates in speed reading are illusory (see E. J. Gibson and H. Levin, The Psychology of Reading, [Cambridge, Mass., 1975], pp. 539-49).

thinkers.name: G. Sperling
thinkers.key_idea: A model for visual memory tasks and processing rates of syllables.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Sperling's findings on processing 10 syllables per second as convergent evidence for a 200ms-per-word processing limit.
key_passages: Sperling has shown that the maximal processing rate for highly familiar syllable sequences is about ten syllables per second

thinkers.name: W. E. Hick
thinkers.key_idea: Hick's Law: Reaction time is a function of the logarithmic number of possible stimuli.
thinkers.source_work: On the Rate of Gain of Information (1952)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Hick's mathematical precision regarding uncertainty and reaction time to provide a psychological basis for processing effort in reading.
key_passages: It was first given precision by W. E. Hick, who demonstrated in 1952 that reaction time increased linearly as the logarithm of the number of possible stimuli

thinkers.name: D. E. Broadbent
thinkers.key_idea: The correlation between reaction time and the subject's sense of signal probability.
thinkers.source_work: Word Frequency Effect and Response Bias
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Broadbent's 'response bias' concept to explain why predictable prose is processed more quickly.
key_passages: Broadbent... proposed an explanation of this 'response bias' by suggesting a correlation between reaction time and the subject’s sense of a signal’s probability.

thinkers.name: H. W. Hake
thinkers.key_idea: Humans act against long-term expectancy by guessing unlikely events in frequency tests.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Hake's experimental data to argue that humans are biologically predisposed to favor local patterns over general ones.
key_passages: Hake has shown that when subjects are asked to guess which of two events will occur... they will guess the unlikely event 20% of the time

thinkers.name: Herbert Spencer
thinkers.key_idea: Poetry is easier to read than prose because its rhythm is more predictable.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch validates Spencer's observation and uses it to critique composition handbooks for neglecting prose rhythm.
key_passages: Herbert Spencer suggested that poetry is often easier to read than prose because of the greater predictability of its rhythm. If that is correct, as I believe it is...

thinkers.name: Carroll and Freedle
thinkers.key_idea: Covert semantic assumptions and presuppositions are fundamental to language comprehension.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their work to validate the existence of the 'invisible context' or 'iceberg' of tacit knowledge in prose.
key_passages: The character of these “covert semantic assumptions and presuppositions” has been well-described by Carroll and Freedle, who call for more research into this subject.

thinkers.name: W. Thomson
thinkers.key_idea: Speech patterns are isochronous and rhythmical.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Thomson to support the idea that rhythm creates local verbal constraints and expectancies.
key_passages: indicated that actual speech patterns tend to be isochronous, suggesting that we tend to continue rhythmical patterns in the production as well as in the reception of speech.

thinkers.name: Ernst Cassirer
thinkers.key_idea: Tracing the insight that the clause is the primary unit of speech back to von Humboldt.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Cassirer to provide historical and philosophical pedigree for the idea of clause primacy.
key_passages: Cassirer, discussing all known language types in his Philosophy of Symbolic Forms in 1953, traced the insight back to von Humboldt.

thinkers.name: Wilhelm von Humboldt
thinkers.key_idea: The clause or clause-sentence as the primary unit of speech.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Humboldt as the originator of the 'old idea' that the clause is the primary linguistic unit.
key_passages: traced the insight back to von Humboldt.

thinkers.name: T. G. Bever
thinkers.key_idea: The clause is the primary perceptual unit and clauses are perceived more directly than words.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Bever's research on the 'paradox' of clause perception to build his scanning-plus-review model.
key_passages: the paradoxical finding that “the clause is the primary perceptual unit.”

thinkers.name: D. B. Orr, H. L. Friedman, and J. C. Williams
thinkers.key_idea: Listening comprehension of speeded discourse has a ceiling that cannot be elevated by training.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their 1965 study to prove that the 300 wpm limit is a fundamental human constraint, not a skill deficiency.
key_passages: Even after practice with rapid listening-rates, people cannot accurately understand rapid discourse above a definite rate.

thinkers.name: William James
thinkers.key_idea: The distinction between primary memory (the just-past) and secondary memory (recollection of things absent from consciousness).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adopts James's distinction as the foundation for the short-term/long-term memory divide that governs readability.
key_passages: In 1890 William James called it “elementary” or “primary” memory and described its essential character better than anyone since

thinkers.name: George A. Miller
thinkers.key_idea: Human short-term memory is limited to roughly seven items (the magic number seven).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Miller's work to establish that memory is precisely limited, though he notes that for language specifically, the number may be lower.
key_passages: The most famous and interesting account of this limitation is Miller’s article “The Magic Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two.”

thinkers.name: Victor Yngve
thinkers.key_idea: Linguistic 'depth'—the cognitive load of grammatical relationships—is limited by the capacity of short-term memory.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Yngve's work as the bridge between psychological memory limits and linguistic structure/readability.
key_passages: This principle has been ably argued in a brilliant and pathbreaking article by Victor Yngve, who uses the term depth for the number of relationships which any grammatical structure requires a person to keep in short-term memory.

thinkers.name: Rommetveit
thinkers.key_idea: Communication efficiency can sometimes be improved by hierarchical constructions.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Rommetveit's experiment to qualify his argument, showing that reducing subordination isn't a universal fix for readability.
key_passages: Under certain conditions, the communication of meaning is improved by hierarchical constructions, as Rommetveit showed...

thinkers.name: Forster and Ryder
thinkers.key_idea: Hypotheses about sense and syntax are pursued serially during sentence perception.
thinkers.engagement: Uses their research to support the claim that memory load is affected by grammatical structure.
key_passages: Recently, Forster and Ryder have supplied evidence 'that hypotheses about sense and syntax are pursued serially,'

thinkers.name: St. Augustine
thinkers.key_idea: Temporal actions (like reciting a psalm) involve a transition where 'expectation' (future) is shortened and 'memory' (past) is enlarged until the whole is nontemporal.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Augustine's description to provide a psychological and philosophical foundation for the idea that reading involves transforming temporal sequences into nontemporal structures of meaning.
key_passages: In discourse, this accommodating of the temporal to the nontemporal was first described by a great early psychologist, St. Augustine

thinkers.name: P. N. Johnson-Laird
thinkers.key_idea: Syntactic structure is lost to memory within seconds and does not represent how meaning is stored.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Johnson-Laird to prove that the 'linguistic object' theory of memory is empirically false.
key_passages: Johnson-Laird stated: 'all forms of syntactic structure are normally lost to memory within a few seconds.'

thinkers.name: Levelt and Kempen
thinkers.key_idea: Doubts the linguistic presupposition that memory stores 'some sort of linguistic object.'
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their 1975 review to show the scientific consensus moving away from linguistic models of memory.
key_passages: express doubt that the implicit assumption of earlier linguistic work can now be maintained—'namely the presupposition that what is stored in memory is some sort of linguistic object.'

thinkers.name: Walter Kintsch
thinkers.key_idea: Quantitative propositional analysis of content as a predictor of reading rate and retention.
thinkers.source_work: The Representation of Meaning in Memory (1974)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Kintsch's experimental data to provide empirical confirmation for his theoretical deductions about thematic tags and working memory constraints.
key_passages: Kintsch’s work seems to me the best and most promising work on extended discourse to be found in the psychological literature, and its results to date powerfully reinforce my deductions

thinkers.name: M. A. K. Halliday
thinkers.key_idea: The psycholinguistic standpoint of language structure and emphasis.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Halliday for a good discussion of emphasis as a psychological factor in language function.
key_passages: A good discussion of emphasis from a psycholinguistic standpoint can be found in M. A. K. Halliday

thinkers.name: J. A. Fodor and M. Garrett
thinkers.key_idea: Explicit relative pronouns improve the speed and durability of sentence processing.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their work as empirical support for his theory that advance information (markers) improves readability.
key_passages: The importance of this advance information has been shown in experiments which compare the processing of two otherwise identical sentences, one with and one without an explicit who or which...

thinkers.name: Alan Moorehead
thinkers.key_idea: Uses a balanced style that provides sufficient context for readers without over-explaining common knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Moorehead's prose as a 'golden mean' example of readability between the extremes of terseness and over-explicitness.
key_passages: Between these two extremes is the sentence that Alan Moorehead actually wrote

thinkers.name: Brand Blanshard
thinkers.key_idea: Style involves a balance where lucidity is highly valued but complex meanings may require complex styles.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Blanshard to support the idea that 'easy' writing is not always possible or desirable given the complexity of the subject matter.
key_passages: This truth is summed up very well by Brand Blanshard in his elegant book on Philosophical Style

thinkers.name: Thomas Babington Macaulay
thinkers.key_idea: The most consistently lucid writer in the history of English literature.
thinkers.engagement: Cited via Blanshard as the gold standard for unfailing lucidity in English prose.
key_passages: The most unfailingly lucid writer in the history of English literature is Macaulay

actors.name: E. B. White
actors.context: Cited as an example of a successful writer who treats the craft as intuitive.

actors.name: William Tilden
actors.context: Cited as a practitioner who derived philosophical principles from physical craft.

actors.name: Sticht

actors.name: T. G. Sticht
actors.context: Researcher who found no difference between reading and listening scores.

actors.name: Early researchers
actors.context: Mentioned as having used simple correlation techniques that yielded misleading results regarding language variables and difficulty.

actors.name: Thorndike-Lorge
actors.context: Creators of the word-frequency list that Hirsch criticizes as less relevant than local predictability for measuring readability.

actors.name: W. R. Garner
actors.context: Cited for his work on uncertainty and structure as psychological concepts.

actors.name: E. R. F. W. Crossman
actors.context: Cited for empirical work showing that predictability reduces reaction time.

actors.name: G. A. Miller
actors.context: Psychologist providing empirical data on sequential constraints in language.

actors.name: H. W. Hake
actors.context: Information theorist/psychologist providing data on frequency expectancy.

actors.name: Herbert Spencer
actors.context: Philosopher/writer whose insight on rhythm is used to support Hirsch's theory.

actors.name: John Carroll
actors.context: Researcher in language comprehension and covert semantic assumptions.

actors.name: Roy Freedle
actors.context: Researcher in language comprehension and covert semantic assumptions.

actors.name: W. Thomson
actors.context: Author of 'The Rhythm of Speech' (1923).

actors.name: D. E. Broadbent
actors.context: Psychologist whose work on perception and communication provides empirical basis for the limits of attention.

actors.name: Herbert Spencer

actors.name: T. G. Bever
actors.context: Psycholinguist whose work on the 'clause as perceptual unit' forms the basis of the chapter's cognitive model.

actors.name: D. B. Orr
actors.context: Researcher whose studies on speeded discourse provide empirical limits for language processing.

actors.name: William James

actors.name: George A. Miller

actors.name: Victor Yngve
actors.context: Linguist who connected memory limits to grammatical structure through 'depth'.

actors.name: Victor Yngve
actors.context: Computational linguist who provided the 'depth' hypothesis.

actors.name: Forster and Ryder
actors.context: Researchers providing evidence for serial processing of syntax.

actors.name: Forster and Ryder
actors.context: Researchers providing empirical evidence for serial language processing.

actors.name: Rommetveit
actors.context: Psycholinguist whose experiments on structure and cohesiveness support the author's nuanced view of readability.

actors.name: George Miller
actors.context: Psychologist whose work on short-term memory capacity is fundamental to the author's theory.

actors.name: George A. Miller
actors.context: Psychologist whose work on communication and chunking provides the foundation for memory load theories.

actors.name: Thomas Babington Macaulay
actors.context: Writer used as a model of highly readable periodic prose.

actors.name: German language planners (17th-18th centuries)
actors.context: Group responsible for imposing the verb-at-the-end rule, which Hirsch considers a detriment to the language's efficiency.

actors.name: T.G. Bever
actors.context: Cited for his model of speech perception and clause processing.

actors.name: St. Augustine
actors.context: Cited for his early psychological insights into memory and temporal sequence.

actors.name: Binet and Henri
actors.context: Psychologists who conducted early research in 1894 on the memory of phrases and ideas.

actors.name: J. S. Sachs
actors.context: Researcher who demonstrated the rapid decay of syntactic memory (active vs. passive) relative to semantic memory.

actors.name: P. N. Johnson-Laird
actors.context: Cited for empirical data on the perception and memory of sentences.

actors.name: J. M. Levelt
actors.context: Cited for research on syntactic and semantic aspects of remembering sentences.

actors.name: J. Keenan
actors.context: Co-author of the 1973 Kintsch study on reading rate.

actors.name: Scientific American
actors.context: Source of text passages used in Kintsch's experiments.

actors.name: Composition Teachers
actors.context: Those who traditionally teach the 'trinity' of unity, coherence, and emphasis without necessarily knowing their psychological bases.

actors.name: Abraham Lincoln
actors.context: Author of the Gettysburg Address, used as a model of readability and semantic depth.

actors.name: U.S. Treasury
actors.context: Mentioned in the example regarding the two-dollar bill.

actors.name: University of Virginia
actors.context: Mentioned in the example regarding the Rotunda reconstruction.

actors.name: Legal Writers
actors.context: Used as an example of writing where excessive local constraints and qualifications delay closure and overtax memory.

actors.name: Seventh-year students
actors.context: Used as a benchmark for the level of education where certain explicit contexts become redundant.

actors.name: Augustine Birrell
actors.context: An essayist quoted by Blanshard regarding the limitations of Macaulay's style.

objections_raised.objection: Standard psychological research on isolated sentences shows that certain structures (like actives and affirmatives) are always easier to process.
objections_raised.response: These findings are limited by their atomistic focus and can be reversed when the same structures are placed within the context of connected discourse.

objections_raised.objection: The Cloze method is impractical because it requires testing a text on subjects rather than predicting readability in advance.
objections_raised.response: While impractical as a substitute for formulas in the drafting stage, the Cloze method is a more sensitive indicator of actual readability and reveals the underlying psychological mechanisms.

objections_raised.objection: A shorter sentence is always more readable or efficient than a longer version.
objections_raised.response: A longer sentence can be more readable if it increases predictability and reduces processing effort, such as by including relative pronouns ('that') that clarify syntax.

objections_raised.objection: The cloze method might fail to consider syntactical complexity, which traditional readability formulas do address.
objections_raised.response: This is a theoretical defect only; in practice, ease of syntax and word predictability are highly correlated. Predictability already accounts for syntactical category expectations.

objections_raised.objection: Linguistic experiments using isolated words or sentences are often used to define meaning or processing.
objections_raised.response: These experiments are unreliable because the inherent semantic uncertainty of an isolated sentence is indefinitely great and varies wildly between individuals without context.

objections_raised.objection: Why do humans act against long-term expectancy in probability tests, given that it is statistically sub-optimal?
objections_raised.source: Implicitly raised by Hirsch based on Hake's data
objections_raised.response: This behavior is biologically useful because short-term probabilities of a local experience are usually more reliable predictors of the immediate future than general long-term probabilities.

objections_raised.objection: If written language lacks situational context, a writer should maximize clarity by making all constraints and knowledge explicit in the text.
objections_raised.response: Excessive explicitness makes prose unreadable, similar to legal documents; readability actually depends on correctly assuming what an audience already knows tacitly.

objections_raised.objection: If we take in the words of a clause one after another, how can the clause itself be the primary perceptual unit rather than the words?
objections_raised.response: Readers suspend final syntactic-semantic decisions about individual words until they achieve semantic closure at the end of the clause.

objections_raised.objection: The scanning-plus-review model is too simple because some semantic decisions occur at the phrase level.
objections_raised.response: Even if closure is partly achieved at the phrase level, the process remains a review process where provisional interpretations are continually updated.

objections_raised.objection: The distinction between short-term and long-term memory is blurred or invalid.
objections_raised.response: The distinction remains highly durable despite these efforts to blur its boundaries.

objections_raised.objection: Readability is simply a direct function of clause length (number of words).
objections_raised.response: Clause length only matters because very brief clauses don't tax memory; however, long clauses can be readable if structure is optimized, and short ones can be unreadable if poorly structured.

objections_raised.objection: Reducing grammatical subordination and hierarchy always improves readability.
objections_raised.source: Commonly inferred from Yngve's work or simplification of Hirsch's thesis.
objections_raised.response: Under certain conditions, hierarchical constructions actually improve communication and identification tasks (e.g., Rommetveit's experiment).

objections_raised.objection: Readability is always enhanced by simply reducing grammatical subordinations.
objections_raised.response: Under certain conditions, hierarchical structures are more effective because they provide greater semantic cohesiveness and integrated units.

objections_raised.objection: Periodic sentences are inherently unreadable because they strain short-term memory.
objections_raised.response: They can be readable if they provide subclausal closure and high predictability, effectively acting like a paragraph and moving cohesion to long-term memory.

objections_raised.objection: Psychological research should focus on the 'onward march' of single sentences/clauses because they can be tested precisely.
objections_raised.response: While single-clause experiments are more precise, ignoring the 'frontier' where clauses are integrated into extended discourse misses the primary factor in prose readability.

objections_raised.objection: Memory of discourse consists of storing some sort of linguistic object or trace of the syntax.
objections_raised.response: Decisive experimental results (Sachs, Johnson-Laird, Brewer) show syntactic form is lost in seconds and synonym substitution is common, proving memory is abstract and non-linguistic.

objections_raised.objection: The recommendation to use a small number of thematic tags might be seen as endorsing a specific repetitive or simplistic style over others.
objections_raised.response: The advice is not based on stylistic preference but on a fundamental psychological principle of representation necessary for any complex thought to be processed by human memory.

objections_raised.objection: Thematic tags and prospective links are artificial compared to natural speech.
objections_raised.response: Writing is inherently artificial; integrative devices are 'natural' to the medium of writing because it lacks the immediate context of speech.

objections_raised.objection: Reducing thematic tags might deplete the richness of the subsurface implications of the text.
objections_raised.response: While representation (the said) is the soul of speech, there must be a compromise so that enough of the 'underside of the iceberg' is visible to ensure security of communication.

objections_raised.objection: Maximum readability can be achieved by following a simple formula of short clauses and familiar words.
objections_raised.response: This leads to monotony and boredom, which fatigues the reader's attention and causes the mind to wander, ultimately increasing processing time.

objections_raised.objection: A memory-taxing, longer description might seem to be less readable than two shorter ones.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch argues that if the longer version uses markers ('that') to create boundaries, it can actually be processed faster and integrated better than the shorter versions.

objections_raised.objection: A writer should always aim for the most rapid style possible.
objections_raised.source: Implicit stylistic advice common in some circles
objections_raised.response: Rapid style might work for a small, homogeneous audience but can baffle and confuse a larger, uninitiated audience if the meaning is complex and needs more context.

objections_raised.objection: Is easy, lucid, and highly readable writing always the highest goal of a writer?
objections_raised.response: No. Sometimes the meaning is complex and requires a difficult style to remain true to the facts; however, the writer must still maintain 'linearity' so the reader isn't forced to reread.
